title: Feline chronic pain management with concurrent disease and drug conflict
presenting_complaint: Declining mobility and quality of life. Owner reports the cat is no longer jumping to favorite perches and is less interactive than 6 months ago.
history: Long-standing osteoarthritis (OA) diagnosed radiographically 18 months ago; previously on meloxicam 0.025 mg/kg PO q24h
Chronic kidney disease (CKD) Stage 2 diagnosed 8 months ago; meloxicam discontinued at that time by primary care veterinarian
Current medications: amlodipine 0.625 mg PO q24h for hypertension, benazepril 0.5 mg/kg PO q24h for CKD management
Appetite is maintained but reduced; mild weight loss of 0.3 kg over 4 months
Owner describes intermittent vocalization at night
No obvious lameness but crouched posture and reluctance to groom hindquarters noted
Owner willing to administer multiple medications if needed
physical_exam: Mild muscle mass loss over lumbar epaxial region and hindlimbs
Stiff gait; reduced extension in bilateral hip and stifle joints on passive range of motion
Lumbar spondylosis palpable on spinal exam
Heart rate: 176 bpm — within feline normal but upper range
Blood pressure: 148 mmHg systolic — borderline controlled on current amlodipine dose
Oral cavity: mild dental tartar, no active stomatitis
Coat: unkempt caudally; cat unable to reach lumbar and tail base for grooming
diagnostics_available: Skeletal radiographs: bilateral hip OA (moderate), thoracolumbar spondylosis (mild to moderate), bilateral stifle OA (mild)
disallowed_shortcuts: Refusing all analgesic intervention because of CKD without considering risk-benefit
Restarting meloxicam at previous dose without modification or monitoring plan
Treating hypertension as resolved without rechecking given borderline current reading
Ignoring quality of life as a primary outcome measure

model_answer_framework.summary: Candidate should recognize that untreated chronic pain in CKD cats is a quality of life crisis and that withholding all analgesia is not a defensible specialist position. Expected approach includes consideration of low-dose NSAID with intensive monitoring, buprenorphine for acute pain episodes, gabapentin for chronic pain and spondylosis, environmental modifications, weight management, and consideration of physical rehabilitation.
